[112:11]Good evening, everybody. You're watching America first. My name is Nicholas J. Fuentes. We

[112:16]have a great show for you tonight. Very excited to be back here with you tonight on Wednesday.

[112:22]We have a lot to talk about tonight, lots to get into. Big show. It's actually kind

[112:36]It was the first night in a long time

[112:39]when I've actually been white-pilled.

[112:41]Every black pill, after all, is really for us a white pill.

[112:47]But tonight it's a little bit more low-key.

[112:51]Tonight we're gonna talk a little bit more

[112:52]about Trump's Gaza deal.

[112:55]And we didn't talk about this a ton yesterday,

[112:59]but I had an idea about this yesterday.

[113:04]I think that the Gaza deal was basically a ploy.

[113:09]And we'll talk tonight about some of the details

[113:12]that we've learned about his proposal.

[113:15]Of course, yesterday, Trump met with Netanyahu.

[113:18]They held a joint press conference

[113:20]and Trump announced his intention

[113:22]to acquire the Gaza Strip, deport everybody from it,

[113:28]bulldoze it, clean it up, and then turn it into a world city.

[113:33]ostensibly, as a U.S. territory.

[113:36]And the proposal was completely unexpected and fresh and different.

[113:42]And I like ideas like that.

[113:45]But of course, many people criticize this because it would most likely entail U.S. soldiers

[113:51]involved in a security role.

[113:54]What people call boots on the ground, it would involve the U.S. military deploying

[114:01]to probably carry out the deportations in itself

[114:06]and then possibly a counterinsurgency

[114:09]like what we saw in Iraq or Afghanistan.

[114:13]And as people pointed out,

[114:15]it would be worse actually than Iraq and Afghanistan

[114:19]because ostensibly, the counterinsurgency in Iraq

[114:22]and Afghanistan had a pretext of a US interest being served.

[114:29]There was at least a front that we were carrying out those conflicts for the benefit of American

[114:35]citizens.

[114:36]If we were to do the same thing in Gaza, there would be no pretext at all.

[114:40]It would be plain and simple, the United States deploying for the sake of Israel's security.

[114:47]And we did that before, but again, this time there's not even an excuse.

[114:55]From me from the American right from the international community from the Arab countries neighboring Israel and Gaza

[115:04]Despite what Trump said in the press conference

[115:06]Saudi Arabia is sticking to their demands for Palestinian statehood

[115:11]Before they move forward with diplomacy with Israel on the Abraham Accords or a number of other things

[115:18]Jordan Egypt

[115:21]reaffirmed that they are unwilling to receive refugees from Gaza.

[115:26]And even the Trump administration itself is walking back the proposal.

[115:31]The press office, the State Department, the Envoy to the Middle East are all saying that

[115:36]there will be no boots on the ground and actually no American money will be spent.

[115:42]And they're walking back other parts of it.

[115:45]And we've also learned that the proposal was not disclosed in advance to any of Trump's

[115:51]senior defense or diplomatic officials, nor even to the Israelis.

[115:57]And I had a hunch yesterday, and if you watched my show from last night, excuse me, I said,

[116:09]I think the end goal is to remove the Palestinians.

[116:13]I think that is one aspect of this that is inevitable at this point.

[116:19]But whether it is under the control of the United States or an international trust, which is what is written in Netanyahu's Gaza 2035 plan,

[116:28]or whether it is a consortium of Arab countries, it's really not relevant.

[116:46]Israel, whether formally or unofficially, I think that's the end goal.

[116:56]and the administration's reaction to the international and domestic reaction and what I think the

[117:03]real purpose of it is.

[117:05]And I think that this is a very important principle and we'll talk about it tonight.

[117:10]When you listen to Trump on foreign policy,

[117:14]you can never take anything he says at face value.

[117:19]And I've said this repeatedly throughout the year

[117:22]and over the many years I've been doing this show,

[117:25]whether it was Brinksmanship with North Korea

[117:28]or the tariff threats on Friday and Saturday,

[117:33]the diplomacy surrounding Canada and Greenland,

[117:37]you can't always take it.

[117:40]To heart precisely the words he's saying because most of the time at least in the early stages

[117:47]What Trump says is supposed to set a frame for a negotiation

[117:53]And that's why typically it's bold and shocking and outrageous and sounds unrealistic because

[118:00]it is a starting point in a negotiation.

[118:04]And that means a few things.

[118:05]It means that one, as a starting point, it is intended to frame the negotiation between

[118:11]two or three or more parties.

[118:19]Whatever your first proposal is, that sets the tone for the counter proposal and then

[118:27]your response to that.

[118:30]On top of that, it also has a psychological effect, and I think that Trump's

[118:35]proposals or actions, which are usually unpredictable or surprising or aggressive, they all have

[118:43]the same pattern, which is they are intended to knock somebody off a balance. It's supposed

[118:48]to, whether they're allies or adversaries, it is supposed to interrupt.

[118:55]They're thinking on the negotiation. I think that those are the two major characteristics

[119:01]Whenever Trump approaches foreign policy again and those initial stages in the beginning

[119:06]It always is characterized by those two attributes. So

[119:22]It's actually good. I like it. It's not, you know, I don't really care that much about

[119:52]that we've abolished the concept of gender.

[119:55]And so it's bathrooms, it's children's sports,

[119:58]it's anything where there is gender segregation.

[120:03]where it's extremely obvious

[120:06]where ideology conflicts with biology.

[120:17]But if you're talking about children sports,

[120:20]there's obvious physical differences

[120:24]and biological advantages and disadvantages.

[120:33]that much about girl sports,

[120:34]it's just one among another or a series of issues

[120:39]where the transgender issue really comes to the front.

[120:43]And I have to say, I like seeing this only because you're starting to see a return to

[120:49]normalcy in the country.

[120:51]We're in this weird transition where it seems that wokeness, it's obviously receding.

[120:57]It's obviously going in a different direction.

[121:08]and it was expanding, moving forward.

[121:11]Now, it seems to have at the minimum stopped

[121:14]and is incrementally moving backwards.

[121:17]I don't know how far it will move backwards,

[121:19]but it is moving backwards.

[121:21]That much is indisputable,

[121:22]and I think that's a positive development.

[121:25]And the reason that it's refreshing is because

[121:27]when we imagine the society that we want to live in,

[121:31]when we try to remember why we're angry,

[121:34]why we're resentful and disillusioned

[121:37]with the society we live in,

[121:39]it's because maybe the most obvious reason,

[121:44]the most apparent reason is because it's not normal.

[121:47]And this is one of the most abnormal things

[121:49]that we have to deal with.

[121:50]It's one of the strangest, most absurd,

[121:53]most abnormal, non-normative things

[121:55]that we have to see now and have to accommodate.

[121:59]But it's sort of interesting that it seems

[122:01]that this is the only way in which our society

[122:04]is becoming more normal.

[122:06]It seems that in every other way,

[122:08]things are becoming less normal,

[122:10]or the ways in which they're not normal

[122:12]have now become the norm.

[122:15]And I think specifically about race and it seems like people have completely forgotten about race all together when I think about why I resist the changes that are happening in the country.

[122:28]First and foremost among them is that the country has become so much more racially diverse and I've talked about it recently on the show.

[122:40]to be an alien in your own country in terms of your culture, physical appearance,

[122:46]everything about your heritage, everything about your ancestry. And it seems like that is something that is completely

[122:55]unaffected by this end of wokeness. And so I question how much wokeness is really on its way out.

[123:03]It seems that, like I've said before,

[123:06]The most extreme fringe types of wokeness are being curbed.

[123:13]But that doesn't mean that wokeness has ended.

[123:16]It actually means that wokeness succeeded.

[123:19]On the contrary.

[123:20]And I think that's an unpopular position.

[123:23]Everybody believes that we won and wokism is a figment of,

[123:28]or I should say something from the past.

[123:31]It's something that's been discarded.

[123:34]I don't believe that's the case.

[123:36]I think that wokeness achieved its objectives.

[123:39]I think that we are all more woke

[123:41]than we were 15 years ago.

[123:43]Everybody is, by a lot.

[123:46]And I think that now that those attitudes are universal,

[123:54]And there's a difference.

[123:55]The woke attitudes remain,

[123:58]but the over the top,

[124:00]enforcement of those attitudes, the aggressive application of those attitudes,

[124:18]think we're all more woke than we were before all of this started and I don't think that's changing at least

[130:45]unorganized thoughts here, but yesterday I looked up Tucker Carlson's book. He put out

[131:55]about this MAGA Trump era.

[131:59]And there's always this insinuation

[132:08]that driving the country to a revolution is a bad thing.

[132:39]that come from...

[133:14]and there's a subtle difference,

[134:25]They see it as a wake-up call for their people to get it together to prevent a revolution

[134:32]They see it as mismanagement which has caused the plebs to get too angry

[134:39]And that sort of fits with this idea that I've been pushing out there really since Trump

[134:44]left office, which is that the establishment is seeking a compromise with the base.

[134:50]Four years ago, the base was ready to burn down Washington and they didn't believe the

[134:55]elections mattered or that our votes were counted.

[134:58]They thought that the media was lying.

[135:01]It was the fertile soil for a revolution.

[135:06]And I think that somewhere along the way the establishment realized that they couldn't

[135:11]silence those people or jail those people or repress those people.

[135:16]So they had to accommodate them or at least accommodate them on the surface.

[135:22]And so there was a handshake between the establishment and the base.

[135:27]And I think that initially that handshake, that compromise was called Ron DeSantis.

[135:33]Maybe before that it was called JD Vance when he was being elevated in 2016.

[135:40]And I think when they realized that they weren't getting rid of Trump, that the Trump phenomenon

[135:46]was unstoppable, I think they realized that they would have to settle for Trump.

[135:52]That a new Trump 2024 would be that handshake.

[136:01]This is born out in the data. In 2020, like 80% of Republicans believe that their votes were

[136:13]So, in four years, all Republicans went from not believing in elections to once again believing

[136:19]in elections.

[137:04]It's completely legitimate.

[138:18]legitimacy was crumbling.

[171:56]This kind of stuff happens all the time in the conservative space, in our new Trump administration,

[172:03]in Silicon Valley, and Donald Trump and Elon Musk are tolerant of it.

[172:10]They're tolerant of it and they do not take a strong moral stance against it.

[172:17]Trump is vaguely, generically Christian, culturally Christian, Elon Musk, I believe

[172:25]is an atheist or an agnostic and has no affinity or relationship with Christianity at all.

[172:33]And so as a consequence, they don't see this as a moral issue.

[172:42]And that's why the regulations ban transgender surgeries

[172:45]for very young kids.

[172:47]And that's why the regulations pertain to sports for children

[172:51]or just sports altogether.

[172:54]But in a sense, what they're really doing

[172:56]is just rolling back the society to the 2000s, the 1990s.

[173:03]I mean, when did dog day afternoon come out?

[173:05]1969 or something like that?

[173:07]This stuff has been with us for a very long time.

[173:10]It's been going on.

[173:12]and you have people in the government

[173:13]that are tolerating it,

[173:15]that think there's nothing wrong with it.

[173:18]Now, the reason why this is a good thing,

[173:20]one, is because it's moral,

[173:22]but two, also because it's normal.

[173:23]We like to see, and it's not that I oppose these things,

[173:26]I think they're good.

[173:27]My first issue is that I actually don't think

[173:30]our regime is very right-wing at all

[173:32]when it comes to these issues.

[173:33]There's certainly more right-wing

[173:34]than the most extreme left-wing people,

[173:37]but they're not really right-wing at all.

[173:39]They're not very right-wing.

[173:41]The other issue that I have is that we like these policies because they are morally correct or more morally correct

[173:50]And we like these policies because they're normal, but what I fear is happening is that

[173:56]rather than reflecting

[173:59]A retracing of wokeism or a recession of wokeism, it seems like all this is doing is putting

[174:07]a cap on wokeism, and thereby seeding all of the ground that wokeism has won up until

[174:15]this point.

[174:16]And what I mean by that is, you hear this refrain from Republicans all the time.

[174:22]Gays are fine, trannies are fine.

[174:25]The only problem is when they try to force good conservatives to transition their kids.

[174:31]It's like, but everything up until that, we are now acknowledging is totally okay and

[174:35]totally fine, porn, contraceptives, feminism, gay marriage, transgenders, it's all fine

[174:43]right up until the worst of the worst where we've capped it.

[174:50]When I think about the country that I live in is the most offensive thing that is going on

[174:55]that there are transgenders and girl sports or that our neighborhoods are completely alien to us because of racial diversity.

[175:07]Honestly, it's the latter for me. And I say that, people might say, well, that's not very Christian of you.

[175:15]or something, but I'm telling you how I feel.

[175:18]I'm telling you, this is my gut feeling.

[175:21]I feel worse that our neighborhoods

[175:24]are becoming unrecognizable because of the languages

[175:27]they speak, because of the way that they look,

[175:30]because of the culture that they have,

[175:32]because of the places that they're from,

[175:34]the fact that they're completely foreign.

[175:36]That feels worse to me than that there's one transgender

[175:40]person and that there's one gay person.

[175:43]because that's really what the statistics say.

[175:46]It's like 1% of the population is trans,

[175:48]it's like 3% is gay.

[175:51]And it seems like this is where woke is,

[175:54]this is where we're getting off,

[175:57]we're getting off at this station from the woke train.

[176:01]And I don't know if I'm really happy about where we are.

[176:04]It's like we've accepted multiracialism,

[176:06]we've accepted feminism,

[176:08]we've accepted all of this other sexual,

[176:13]perversity or promiscuity or degeneracy,

[176:16]but we're just going to cap it off

[176:18]at some of the worst of the worst.

[176:19]And it's not to say that those aren't good developments.

[176:21]They are.

[176:22]It's good that they're rolling these things back.

[176:25]And it's certainly good for the children

[176:26]that would have been affected by it.

[176:28]So there is a material good here.

[176:31]Like real flesh and blood people are benefiting from it.

[176:33]And for that reason, I support it.

[176:35]And there's always a question of obviously rolling

[176:38]all of it back wasn't on the ballot this year.

[176:42]But I think that's why it's very important to keep pushing when people tell me that I'm blackpilling that I'm never happy

[176:49]I'm never satisfied on some level. Yeah, I'm I'm really not happy. I'm not gonna be satisfied until we've gone all the way

[176:57]if the country is 50% non-white and

[177:01]You still have this explosion of degeneracy

[177:05]But we have some of these executive orders like okay the the girl soccer game is gonna look a little bit more normal

[177:12]I'm not happy, I'm not satisfied with that.

[177:14]That's still not the country that I grew up in.

[177:17]And I grew up in the 2000s.

[177:20]Forget about the 90s, 80s, 70s, 60s, 50s, 40s.

[177:23]Forget about the 19th century.

[177:25]I grew up in the 2000s and it's still unrecognizable to me.

[177:29]I grew up when Lady Gaga and Glee

[177:32]were like a cultural phenomenon.

[177:34]And it's unrecognizable to me today

[177:39]after the supposed victory.

[177:42]So, I think that it's very important that our push for normalcy doesn't stop with girl

[177:48]sports and bathrooms.

[177:49]It has to go even further and it has to expand into other areas, like having a country of

[177:55]foreigners, that's not normal either.

[177:58]And that has to change in one or two ways, either you need to shut off the immigration

[178:02]and start deporting people or white people need to be allowed to live in their own neighborhoods.

[178:10]White people need to be able to preserve their culture

[178:15]and find ways to organize and live in a society

[178:18]where we can live among our own kinds of people.

[178:21]And that means that socially it has to be destigmatized

[178:24]and that means that legally it has to be made legal.

[178:30]It needs to stop being prohibited from happening.

[178:33]because I don't know about you guys,

[178:35]but I mean, all this, everything that's going on,

[178:38]it is not changing the fundamental issue,

[178:41]which is that we are living in an alien nation.

[178:45]And that's why Jared Taylor said,

[178:46]I don't know how much of a victory Trumpism is

[178:49]for the white people anymore.

[178:50]It's a victory in some ways,

[178:52]but you cannot call it a victory for white identity,

[178:55]strictly speaking.

[178:57]And as a Catholic,

[178:59]you can't call it a victory for Christianity either.

[179:03]I mean, we're getting some stuff out of it, but it's not really delivering anything that

[179:10]feels very fulfilling.

[179:11]So anyway, so that's that, but I do want to move on into our big story tonight.

[179:14]I want to talk a little bit more about the Gaza proposal from Trump yesterday.

[179:19]And of course, that was our big story last night.

[179:23]Donald Trump hosted BB Netanyahu at the White House

[179:26]first foreign visit of his presidency.

[179:29]And they talked about a lot of things,

[179:31]including potential strikes on Iran.

[179:34]They talked about the Abraham Accords.

[179:37]And they talked about the ceasefire in Gaza.

[179:40]And the big headline from the day

[179:42]was Trump's shocking proposal for the United States

[179:45]to annex the Gaza Strip.

[179:48]and exiling every Palestinian that lives there

[179:51]and turning it into some kind of a smart city,

[179:55]some kind of international city,

[179:57]international free trade zone, international trust,

[180:02]light on the details,

[180:04]and a totally new fresh proposal.

[180:08]But this is what everybody's been talking about

[180:10]for the past day.

[180:11]And we talked all about it last night on the usual stuff.

[180:16]Israel's control over our government,

[180:19]and how there's this deference to Netanyahu,

[180:22]and how he's paying the piper here,

[180:24]he's singing for his supper.

[180:26]Trump got $200 million from the Israel lobby,

[180:29]and now they're coming to collect on that investment.

[180:32]We talked about all the good stuff last night.

[180:35]But tonight, I want to talk specifically about that proposal in itself, because something

[180:40]we didn't talk too much about last night, but which I talked about a little bit, is

[180:45]that I don't think this is a serious proposal.

[180:49]I don't think that when Trump says the United States will own the Gaza Strip, we're gonna

[180:54]clean it up and we're gonna turn it into a territory and it'll be a U.S. city, I don't

[181:03]I don't think that is the real plan.

[181:06]That doesn't mean that it is completely divorced

[181:08]from the real plan.

[181:10]I think it is in the realm of the real plan.

[181:14]It is certainly part of the conversation of the real plan.

[181:17]But I think the big thrust of what was disclosed yesterday

[181:22]is that this was a starting point

[181:24]for a very, very complex negotiation

[181:27]between four or five parties.

[181:31]This is a negotiation between Israel and Hamas.

[181:35]It's a negotiation between the United States and Israel.

[181:39]It's a negotiation between Israel, the United States, and Saudi Arabia.

[181:45]Between Saudi Arabia and the Palestinians.

[181:48]It's a negotiation between the United States and Iran, between Iran and Israel.

[181:53]There are really five maybe six major players here and there are so many moving pieces. It's the most complex deal and

[182:02]Like I said yesterday, it runs through many significant issues. It's not just the Israel Palestinian conflict

[182:10]But it's also the Israel Iranian conflict

[182:15]It's also the Abraham Accords and whether Saudi Arabia and the Gulf countries will

[182:22]continue to entrench their relationship with Israel.

[182:26]And it's a negotiation for the United States

[182:29]of averting a war with Iran,

[182:31]bringing an end to the hostilities in Gaza,

[182:33]and bringing the Gulf countries into a block with Israel

[182:36]to oppose Iran, Russia, and China.

[182:40]So it's a very, very complex deal.

[182:43]And when Donald Trump goes out like he did yesterday and drops a bombshell on everybody,

[182:49]and this is a proposal that is new.

[182:52]This was not a part of the conversation yesterday morning.

[182:56]And it's something that shocks literally everybody.

[182:59]I don't know anybody that's happy with this proposal other than maybe the furthest right

[183:03]elements in the state of Israel.

[183:10]And if you've been following the Trump administration for the past eight years or even the Trump

[183:14]campaigns, this is classic Trump.

[183:17]This is what he does.

[183:19]This is the art of the deal.

[183:20]And it's not necessarily to say that this is five dimensional chess or 64 dimensional

[183:26]chess.

[183:27]It's not to say that there is a convoluted plan that is going to defy reality.

[183:37]Well, I think what Trump excels at is tactics more than strategies.

[183:43]And this is, and so what I mean by that is when we say something like five dimensional

[183:48]chess, 64 dimensional chess, what people think is that there is this very convoluted,

[183:54]very complex, very long-term strategy.

[184:01]that he understands from the beginning that no one else does.

[184:04]And I don't think that's what this is.

[184:07]I think this is a ploy.

[184:09]Rather than a five-dimensional chess play

[184:13]that is unfolding with this move,

[184:16]I think this is a ploy.

[184:19]This is a tactic.

[184:20]And this is what he always does,

[184:23]which is to say when he gets in office

[184:25]and when he starts a negotiation on anything,

[184:28]he drops a bomb.

[184:30]He says something shocking.

[184:33]It gets headlines.

[184:34]It surprises everybody.

[184:37]It's something new, something totally unpredictable.

[184:40]And when he says it, everybody is surprised, angry, and everybody's first reaction is that

[184:47]will never happen.

[184:48]That is unacceptable.

[184:51]And that's the point.

[185:00]And maybe more than anything as a deal maker that puts together complex deals, he understands

[185:05]the psychology of a negotiation between multiple parties.

[185:10]And when Trump goes into the room where everybody says, everybody says, these are our demands

[185:18]and everybody is at an impasse, Hamas wants to remain in power, Israel says Hamas cannot

[185:27]The Palestinians want to stay, Israel wants them to go.

[185:32]Israel wants to deal with Saudi Arabia, but Saudi Arabia won't make a deal unless there's a Palestinian state.

[185:38]Israel says that's unacceptable.

[185:41]The United States want Israel to stop the fighting, but Israel won't stop the fighting until Hamas is eradicated.

[185:47]Hamas can't be eradicated unless the fighting goes on and is more brutal.

[185:51]That's unacceptable to the U.S.

[185:54]The U.S. wants Saudi Arabia to make a deal with Israel, but there's that impasse there.

[185:59]And so there's, there, there is this gridlock here.

[186:05]Everybody is at an impasse.

[186:07]There is this rigid situation where everybody is checking everybody else and nothing can

[186:16]move forward.

[186:18]And that's where Trump comes in and drops a proposal like this and changes the way everybody

[186:27]It also changes what everybody thinks about what is possible or what is likely.

[186:33]Israel says, we're going to go into Gaza and there's nothing anybody can do to stop it.

[186:38]The Palestinians say, well, we're not going to stop fighting and we're going to make it

[186:42]brutal.

[186:42]And Saudi Arabia says, we're not going to move.

[186:45]And what we want is for Palestine or some Arab group to control the strip.

[186:50]If Trump comes in and says, no, we're going to come in and we're going to take the strip.

[186:56]Nobody wants that. Israel doesn't necessarily want that in that form. The Palestinians definitely

[187:03]don't want that. Saudi Arabia doesn't want that. Jordan, Egypt doesn't want that. Iran

[187:08]doesn't want, nobody wants that. So I think this is classic Trump where he comes in and

[187:15]says something that knocks everybody off balance and interrupts their cycle of thinking. He

[187:24]thinking, where they're looking at everybody else. Everybody else is looking at each other.

[187:29]They have expectations. There's this communication relay. Trump is getting in the middle of that

[187:35]process, interrupting it and causing everybody to stop and think again.

[187:42]And by introducing a new option, he's also changing the horizon of possibilities.

[187:47]Everybody thinks there's maybe two options or three options.

[187:51]There is an understandable, a comprehensible set of permutations of how this can play out.

[187:58]And when Trump introduces a fork, a new one, now it changes everybody's horizon of what

[188:06]might be possible.

[188:11]So it's something new there as well.

[188:15]So I think that when Trump says we're going to annex Gaza, I don't believe that strictly

[188:23]speaking will happen.

[188:25]I don't think that America will move the sent com headquarters to Gaza and annex it and

[188:32]make it an American territory.

[188:37]But I think that something like that will happen, but first I want to get into the story and support this a little bit.

[188:46]This is from the New York Times.

[188:49]It says, quote, when President Trump announced his proposal for the United States to take ownership of Gaza on Tuesday, he shocked even senior members of his own White House and government.

[188:59]While his announcement looked formal and thought out, reading from a sheet of paper, his administration

[189:04]had not even done the most basic planning to examine the feasibility of the idea, according

[189:10]to four people and knowledge of the discussions.

[189:13]It wasn't only the American scrambling, the announcement came as just as much of a surprise

[189:17]to the Israeli visitors.

[189:19]Soon before they walked out for their news conference, Mr. Trump surprised Netanyahu,

[189:29]That, in itself, is a surprise.

[189:34]Netanyahu is expecting they're going to go into this press conference and Trump is going

[189:38]to demand that Jordan and Egypt take the Palestinians.

[189:41]And Trump says, hey, hey, listen, they're in front of a hot microphone.

[189:46]The press has gathered.

[189:48]And this is a maker break trip for Netanyahu. This is make or break. If Trump leaves the

[189:54]United States, if he leaves Washington without firm support from Trump, his government may

[189:59]explode when he goes back to Israel. So Netanyahu's there. He needs to project strength. He needs

[190:07]to project stability coming from his coalition government needs to demonstrate that Trump is

[190:15]and Trump goes and says, Hey, man, just real quick,

[190:20]before we take questions, I'm going to announce

[190:22]that the United States will annex the strip.

[190:26]What do you think that Yahoo's reaction is?

[190:30]If you're, and you've been in situations like this,

[190:34]that sinking feeling when your heart drops,

[190:37]you start swimming, thoughts, well, what does that mean?

[190:41]Was he serious?

[190:44]That's the whole point. It's what he does. It's what he does every time

[190:50]That's the put it's a play. It's a cheap

[190:53]Play and we know that because he didn't tell it. It's not a real plan

[190:57]He didn't campaign on that. He didn't talk to his advisors about that. They didn't assess the feasibility

[191:02]There's nothing in the works about that because it's not real

[191:06]That's an idea that popped in his head

[191:09]And all of the, it's really a negative idea in the sense that it's not a positive vision.

[191:19]into the existing framework,

[191:21]into the existing perspective or framing of the topic.

[191:25]And that is meant to change the calculus

[191:28]of all of the players involved

[191:30]and for them to reevaluate what's possible.

[191:34]So when they say Trump was kicking this idea around,

[191:36]but he didn't do any feasibility assessment

[191:39]or any of the usual stuff,

[191:45]boots on the ground in Gaza.

[191:48]if he has anything to say about it, and he might not.

[191:57]to embroil American soldiers in Gaza

[192:01]involved in the ethnic cleansing of the Palestinians.

[192:07]And for that reason, I don't think it's strictly speaking

[192:11]in that way, in that particular way,

[192:14]it's a serious proposal.

[192:16]I think it was a ploy. With that being said, so I'll throw a bone to some of the plan trusters

[192:23]because the plan trusters said he's not serious. Some of that, and there was a spectrum. Some

[192:29]of them said, we're going to take Gaza and it's a good thing. It's actually a good thing

[192:33]because it's going to be a crusader state. It's going to be a confessional state and

[192:48]and you shouldn't take it to face value.

[192:53]but they're not stupid about what Trump does

[193:04]Now, the question is, what is the real plan

[193:07]and why would he say that?

[193:10]Well, there's something interesting.

[193:12]Which is that Netanyahu has a very fragile coalition government in Israel

[193:17]He's got his lycude party and then he has these religious Zionists and they're giving him the votes to have a majority

[193:26]In their legislature in the Knesset

[193:28]So you've got this guy Bezelal Smotrich and Ben Gavir

[193:34]And these are your religious Zionists.

[193:36]These guys are as extreme as it gets.

[193:39]They're open about desiring greater Israel.

[193:42]They do not want the fighting in Gaza to stop

[193:45]until the Palestinians are all gone and the settlers go in.

[193:49]They support Jewish settlements in the West Bank and in Gaza.

[193:53]They think that Israel should be in the Sinai.

[193:55]They think that Israel should take over Jordan.

[193:58]These guys are as radical and bloodthirsty as it gets.

[194:01]And they have said that unless Netanyahu fights the war

[194:05]until Hamas is eradicated, they will pull the plug.

[194:09]They will stop supporting his government

[194:11]and he will lose the majority.

[194:13]And if he loses the majority,

[194:15]he is no longer the prime minister.

[194:17]And if he's no longer the prime minister,

[194:18]he goes to jail because he's being prosecuted right now.

[194:21]So the crisis has to go on for Netanyahu's sake.

[194:25]And it also has to go on to satisfy these extremists so that they will keep his government

[194:31]in power.

[194:32]So when Trump sent his envoy, Steve Witkoff, to Israel to negotiate the ceasefire a couple

[194:40]of weeks ago, it was these ministers, finance and national security minister, that said,

[194:46]we do not support the ceasefire unless we get a commitment that we could start the fighting

[194:51]They opposed the ceasefire, in so far as it represented a commitment to a permanent settlement

[194:57]without destroying Hamas.

[195:00]But once they got reassurance from Netanyahu and presumably from the United States that

[195:06]it was temporary and the fighting would continue, they supported the deal.

[195:11]Now, it would seem that Netanyahu is maybe more realistic, that he knows, just like the

[195:17]military and Israeli intelligence says, they can't kill all of Hamas without really just

[195:22]removing all the Palestinians.

[195:24]Maybe Netanyahu is more realistic about what can really be done here.

[195:28]When Trump says, no, we are going to get rid of all the Palestinians.

[195:33]This charges up the far right in Israel and puts pressure on Netanyahu to do something

[195:41]And so you wonder if maybe this is some way of influencing the internal politics in Israel

[195:48]and creating an impossible situation for Netanyahu.

[195:52]Another way to look at it is that there is a big question mark about what they're going to do about the Palestinians.

[196:00]Will they stay or go?

[196:02]And if they stay, who's going to run Gaza?

[196:07]and presumably it won't be the Palestinian Authority

[196:10]and it won't be the Palestinians themselves.

[196:13]So we'll have to be the Arabs,

[196:15]but Israel doesn't want it to be the Arab countries.

[196:18]They think that's unacceptable.

[196:19]So it's gonna have to be other partners.

[196:22]And these other partners won't come in.

[196:24]The Arabs won't come in and other partners won't come in

[196:26]if the Palestinians are kicked out.

[196:29]So,

[196:30]If they get kicked out, everyone's gonna be angry.

[196:33]It's very untenable.

[196:34]No one will support this diplomatically.

[196:36]It's very difficult.

[196:37]But if they stay, there's a question of who's gonna run it.

[196:41]If it's not the Palestinians, the Arabs won't buy in.

[196:45]The Israelis don't even want the Arabs to buy in.

[196:48]So if the United States comes in and says, well, we're going to run the whole strip.

[196:52]Everybody says, whoa, whoa, whoa, well, Israel wants to run it.

[196:55]The Arabs want to run it.

[196:56]The Palestinians want to stay.

[196:58]Everybody says we reject Palestinians leaving and America running it.

[197:05]So is this meant to encourage and force the Arabs to rethink their negotiating position?

[197:12]Or maybe the Israelis saying, Hey, look, if you're not going to make a deal, then we'll

[197:17]just take it.

[197:18]If the Arabs all make a deal, then we'll, then us and the Israelis will take it.

[197:24]It seems like it's putting pressure on everybody in the region to reconsider their red lines

[197:30]and their demands, because as it stands right now, it seems that Netanyahu is going to resume

[197:35]the fighting, go back in the strip.

[197:38]This is going to be very unpopular for Trump.

[197:41]It's going to postpone any kind of settlement with Saudi Arabia.

[197:49]And the only way that Israel stops the fighting in Gaza is if there's some kind of way where

[197:56]there's administration of the strip that does not fall into the hands of Hamas with some

[198:00]sort of guarantee.

[198:01]And that would need to come from the Arabs and they won't agree unless there's statehood,

[198:06]which Israel doesn't want.

[198:07]So it seems like taking, and it's sort of a complex thing.

[198:14]precisely what Trump is trying to do here,

[198:16]who he's squeezing in particular.

[198:19]But I think in general, the point was to throw out something completely unacceptable

[198:24]to maybe get every other partner, every other party to the negotiation in the region

[198:31]to consider changing their minds about what they will or will not accept in an ultimate settlement.

[198:38]think that's what he intended to do. I think he intended to blow up the

[198:43]discussion, change the frame, hardball Netanyahu or maybe some of the others,

[198:49]although it's not really hardball because ultimately the Israelis want the

[198:53]Palestinians out. And I think even if it belonged to America, I don't know that

[198:57]they would really mind that because the next president would just give it to

[199:01]him. Trump would probably give it to him. So I don't know that they really don't

[199:06]but I don't think America would do that necessarily,

[199:09]although they could.

[199:11]Now, that's the intention.

[199:13]I think though that Netanyahu is used to dealing with Trump.

[199:17]And I think that at this stage in the game,

[199:20]here we are, nine years in, eight years in,

[199:24]10 years, I mean, really, we're 10 years in.

[199:28]I think Netanyahu has seen this before.

[199:30]Okay, this guy's been in power for like 20 years.

[199:33]And when Trump does these cheap tricks, when he throws a, oh, I'm going to take over.

[199:39]I think that some people, some people are rattled by this.

[199:42]The Denmark is rattled because the proposal for Greenland is actually super serious.

[199:47]They've been on the ground there for a long time.
